parts.text:
The bass downstairs is loud enough that it’s vibrating up through the soles of my boots. The hallway smells like stale beer, wet drywall, and cheap cologne. My hair is already curling at the temples—the humidity of two hundred sweating bodies crammed into the ground floor of the Phi Delt house doing the work of an Ohio July, even though it’s mid-October. 

I bite the inside of my cheek. I’m leaning against the wall next to a composite photo from 2026. 

He’s from my environmental ethics seminar. I’ve watched him take notes. I thought I had him figured out. Arrogant, but lazy about it. Safe. I am sixty percent good at reading people. I thought this was the sixty percent.

Going upstairs was an impulsive decision. My social battery was blinking red, Priya was somewhere by the kegs being aggressively radiant, and he said, *Let’s just go up to my room, I have a joint, it’s quieter.* I made the impulsive choice. Now I am trying to be careful about it.

His room is messy. A pile of laundry. A desk covered in unwashed cups. He locks the door, and the click sounds too loud.

When he kisses me, it’s fine. I’m not even that into it, but it’s fine. 

Then it’s not. 

His weight shifts. He pushes me back onto the mattress, and the angle is wrong. His hand goes under my shirt, rough, tearing at the fabric.

"Wait," I say. I pull my mouth away. "Hold on."

He doesn't answer. He just adjusts his grip, pinning my leg with his knee. 

"Hey," I say. My voice is sharp now. "No. Stop."

He doesn't stop. He doesn't even look at my face. It’s like I’m not talking. It’s like the sound of my voice is just background noise, like the bass thumping through the floorboards. 

I push against his chest. I have my dad’s hands. Wide palms. They know how to grip, how to torque a wrench, how to hold a bird without crushing it. I push as hard as I can, but the mechanics of the physics are just math, and I am smaller, and he is heavy, and he doesn't care.

*No,* I say again, but my throat closes around the word. 

I stare at the ceiling. There is a water stain in the corner of the plaster, spreading outward in a jagged ring. It looks like the topographic map of the county where my uncle lives, where the water table is doing something wrong. I focus on the stain. I focus on the rubber band around my wrist, the one I always keep there, pressing hard into my skin, cutting off the circulation. 

When I was fourteen, Kayla told that boy my secret, and I lost my mind over it. I couldn't articulate why I hated her so much for it. It was the fact that I had been turned into a joke, into a *thing*, a prop in someone else's story without my permission. My agency was a punchline. 

This isn't a joke. But it is the exact same math. 

When it’s over, he rolls off me. He breathes heavily, staring at the ceiling. He doesn't say anything. 

I sit up. I fix my clothes. My hands are shaking so badly I can barely manage the button of my jeans. I don't look at him. I unlock the door. I walk down the stairs, through the pulsing, humid mass of people. Nobody stops me. Nobody knows that the world just ended. 

The air outside is freezing. It hits my damp hair and instantly chills my scalp. I start walking. I don't put my headphones in. I don't want boygenius or black midi. I don't want to hear anything. 

My phone buzzes in my pocket. A voice memo from Mel. Probably a two-minute rant about some guy in her lab or the traffic on High Street. I stare at her name on the screen. I can't listen to it. I can't send a text back with a joke. If I hear her voice, my chest will crack open, and I will bleed out right here on the sidewalk.

I let myself into my apartment. Priya isn't home yet. The apartment is completely silent except for the hum of the refrigerator. 

I go to my room. I don't turn on the light. I sit on the edge of my bed. I still have my boots on. I pull the hair tie off my wrist. There is a deep, angry red trench in my skin where it was digging in. I rub my thumb over it. 

I open my phone. I bypass the texts. I open the Notes app.

***

**October 11, 2031**

I was in the forty percent. 
I was so wrong. 

There is a difference between a chickadee and a nuthatch. A chickadee will fly away if you move too fast. A nuthatch climbs face-first down the trunk of a tree. They have different calls. They have different behaviors. If you know what to look for, you are never surprised by what a bird does. Dad says you just have to watch them. 

I watched him and I didn't see it. I didn't see the thing that was going to hurt me. 

I said no. I said it out loud. He heard me. I know he heard me because his jaw tensed but he just kept going. 

I don't know what to do with the fact that I said no and it didn't matter. It didn't change the outcome. Statistically, my voice had zero effect on the environment. I was just a physical object in the room. 

I have to go to class on Tuesday. He sits two rows behind me. 

I want to eat oatmeal. I want to eat my oatmeal and I want it to be tomorrow, or next year. I want to take a shower but I think if I stand under the water I am going to scream and Priya will come home and ask me what's wrong. I can't be warm with her. I can't be anything right now. 

My hands aren't big enough. I pushed and he didn't even move. 

I don't know how to fix this. I don't know who to tell. Mel will drive down here. Mom will panic. Dad won't have a bird fact for this. 

I said no. 
I said no. 
I said no.
